narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad years and prepping for the LSAT—it's like the finish line keeps moving just out of reach. The admissions team uses a predictive tool that looks at patterns from thousands of students who've walked this path before, and for you, it forecasted about a 47% chance of not passing the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales against your application to our program. At the top of what caught its attention was your LSAT score of 31, which stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward that tougher road ahead, more than anything else in your profile. Layered on that was your Year 3 decile of 1, meaning you ranked near the bottom of your class by then, which the tool saw as the second-strongest hint of potential struggles in a demanding law program. Your Year 1 decile of 3 wasn't far behind, reinforcing a picture of grades that started middling and trended downward over time. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.2 and upper-middle family income, those factors nudged the prediction further toward failure in combination, as the tool weighs how they fit into broader success stories. Your background as a white male pursuing full-time studies played smaller roles but still aligned with patterns it's learned to associate with higher risks. It's not that any one thing defines you—it's this blend that painted a cautious picture for the model, one where past academic dips outweighed the positives. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential; it's just a data-driven glimpse based on what similar profiles have faced before. Many students in your shoes have turned things around with targeted prep, like extra LSAT practice or grade-boosting strategies in early law years. If you strengthen those key areas, you could rewrite this story entirely. Hang in there—you've got the drive, and this is just one chapter.
